title: Hawthorn
subtitle: The cardiac tonic of the hedgerow
excerpt: Crataegus monogyna: the heart herb of western and Chinese herbal medicine, traditionally used to support healthy circulation and mild cardiovascular function. Strong study evidence supports its use for mild cardiac insufficiency.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve (the early 20th-century botanical writer), A Modern Herbal, hawthorn entry: the cardiac tradition, the berry and flower preparations. the herbal reference on Crataegi folium cum flore, the well-established evidence for mild cardiac insufficiency (NYHA Class II). the World Health Organisation Monograph on Selected Medicinal Plants, Vol. 2, Flos cum Folio Crataegi.

glossaryTerms.definition: A herb traditionally used to support the heart's function. In western herbal medicine, hawthorn is the primary cardiac tonic, drawing on its flavonoid and oligomeric procyanidin content.
A class of flavonoid polymers found in high concentrations in hawthorn berries and flowers. They are associated with the herb's antioxidant and cardiac-supporting actions.

content.text: Hawthorn (Crataegus monogyna) is the hedgerow tree of Britain and northern Europe. It blazes white with blossom in May and bears deep-red berries (haws) in autumn. It lives for hundreds of years. The leaf-and-flower combination and the berries are both used in herbal medicine. The leaf-and-flower form is the most studied clinically.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Cardiac medication. This is the most important caution for hawthorn: it potentiates the action of digoxin, beta-blockers, and ACE inhibitors. Readers on any cardiac medication must consult their prescriber before using hawthorn at therapeutic doses. The interaction is significant and well-documented.

Diagnosed heart disease. Hawthorn has evidence of benefit in mild cardiac insufficiency (NYHA Class II). If you have diagnosed heart disease, any hawthorn use should be part of a management plan agreed with your cardiologist or GP. Hawthorn is not a substitute for prescribed cardiac medication.

Pregnancy. No safety concerns are identified in the traditional herbal reference at standard doses, but the database is limited. Consult a herbalist or midwife before any therapeutic use in pregnancy.

content.text: Latin binomial: Crataegus monogyna (common hawthorn; also C. laevigata, the Midland hawthorn). Family: Rosaceae. Parts used: leaf with flower (harvested May-June when in blossom), berries/haws (harvested September-October). The leaf-and-flower is the most clinically studied form. The plant's characteristic feature is the thorns (crataegus means strong, from the hardness of the wood) and the five-petalled white flowers, which turn from green-budded to open-white in the first warm days of May.
(OPCs) and flavonoids (vitexin, quercetin and others) are the primary active compounds in the leaf-and-flower and berries. The

action has good evidence behind it. Multiple trials have shown hawthorn extract to improve exercise ability and reduce symptoms in mild heart failure. A large trial (SPICE, 2008) found no benefit for preventing cardiac events. Benefit for symptom relief remains supported by the evidence.

Maud Grieve, the early 20th-century botanical writer, records hawthorn as a heart herb in British folk medicine, noting its use for high blood pressure and heart weakness. The berries were used both as medicine and food. Chinese medicine uses related Crataegus species for digestion and heart support.

Infusion of dried leaf-and-flower: 2-4 g in 150 ml hot water, steeped covered 10-15 minutes. the European herbal standard's reference dose is 3.5-19.8 g of herbal preparation daily depending on the extract form. Spirit extraction (1:5 in 25% spirit, 1-2 ml three times a day) is the most standardised home form. Standardised dry extract capsules are the form used in clinical trials and provide the most reproducible dose. Berry preparations (jelly, syrup from haws) are traditional and wholesome but less standardised.

Cardiac medication: as above, must consult prescriber. Diagnosed heart disease: as above, must be part of an agreed plan. Pregnancy: as above, consult herbalist or midwife. Any sudden worsening of breathlessness, chest pain, ankle swelling, or palpitations needs urgent medical assessment, not herbal management.
